label: World Bank project appraisal parameters (carbon, VSL, shadow pricing)

carbon_values.description: Shadow Price of Carbon for World Bank Group investment project financing. Mandatory for all IFC/IBRD projects since 2017.
carbon_values.note: Low/high range reflects uncertainty in damage estimates. The World Bank recommends using the high value for projects in carbon-intensive sectors and the low value as a minimum floor.
carbon_values.source_detail: World Bank Guidance Note on Shadow Price of Carbon (2017, updated 2024)

vsl.description: Value of Statistical Life by World Bank country income group. Derived using income-elasticity benefit transfer from a US base VSL.
vsl.methodology: Income-elasticity transfer: VSL_country = VSL_US * (GNI_country / GNI_US) ^ elasticity. Elasticity = 1.0 (unit elastic). GNI at Atlas method, not PPP.
vsl.note: These are approximate group averages. For project-specific appraisal, compute VSL using the country's actual GNI per capita and the transfer formula.

shadow_exchange_rate.description: Shadow Exchange Rate Factor (SERF) adjusts for trade distortions. Converts domestic prices to border (world) prices.

low_distortion.note: Open economies with low tariffs (e.g., Singapore, Chile)

moderate_distortion.note: Most middle-income countries with 5-15% average tariffs

high_distortion.note: Countries with significant trade barriers (>15% average tariff)

shadow_exchange_rate.note: SERF > 1.0 means the economic value of foreign exchange exceeds the market exchange rate. Apply SERF to all traded goods inputs/outputs in the economic analysis.

standard_conversion_factor.description: Standard Conversion Factor (SCF) converts market prices of non-traded goods to economic prices.
standard_conversion_factor.note: Apply SCF to non-traded goods (construction labour, local materials, land). Traded goods use border prices directly.

shadow_wage_rate.description: Shadow Wage Rate Factor (SWRF) for unskilled labour in labour-surplus economies.

severe_surplus.note: Very high unemployment/underemployment (>25%). Opportunity cost of labour is near zero. Examples: post-conflict, remote rural areas.

moderate_surplus.note: Significant underemployment (15-25%). Workers have some productive alternative (subsistence agriculture).

mild_surplus.note: Moderate unemployment (5-15%). Workers have reasonable alternative employment.

no_surplus.note: Full employment or skilled labour. Opportunity cost equals market wage.

skilled_labour.note: Skilled and professional labour is always valued at market wage (SWRF = 1.0), regardless of country context.

shadow_wage_rate.note: SWRF = shadow wage / market wage. Multiply market labour costs by SWRF to get economic cost. Lower SWRF means the project creates more net economic value from employing idle labour.

source.publication: Guidelines for the Economic Analysis of Projects (2017); Guidance Note on Shadow Price of Carbon (2017, updated 2024); World Bank Operational Manual

caveats: World Bank appraisal uses ERR as the primary metric, not NPV/BCR. The discount rate is the hurdle rate, not an input to NPV computation.
Shadow pricing (SERF, SCF, SWRF) is essential for developing country CBAs. Market prices systematically misstate economic values due to trade barriers, taxes, and labour market distortions.
VSL by income group is approximate. Always compute country-specific VSL using the transfer formula for formal appraisals.
Carbon values are mandatory for IFC/IBRD projects but lower than UK DESNZ or EIB shadow prices.
